channel.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India
channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: Scientists studied 30 Sierra Nevada ponds that can swing 20°C in a day. Shrinking snowpack may make them smaller, warmer and more nutrient-rich
item.description: Mountain ponds in California's Sierra Nevada show significant daily temperature changes. Winter snowpack profoundly influences pond size and water temperature throughout summer. Less snowfall leads to warmer, smaller ponds with concentrated nutrients. Climate change projections indicate reduced snowpack, impacting these delicate ecosystems. These small water bodies play a crucial role in mountain biodiversity and nutrient cycling.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Quote of the day by Eric Hoffer: 'In times of change, learners inherit the Earth, while the learned find themselves beautifully equipped for a world that no longer exists'
item.description: As technology evolves swiftly, the landscape of industries undergoes significant transformation. Those who are willing to learn and adapt will thrive, while others may find themselves left behind. Continuous education is now a must for both professionals and students. By embracing change and acquiring new skills, individuals unlock new opportunities. Lifelong learning promotes relevance and readiness for the challenges that lie ahead.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Meet the Black Mambas: In one of South Africa's regions hardest hit by poaching, 36 unarmed women patrol the frontline and protect endangered rhinos
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Man steals kitten to rob bank, but gets caught
item.description: A curious case unfolded when a man stole a kitten named Magnolia from a pet store, employing her as a smokescreen during a bank robbery. As he demanded money from the teller, law enforcement swiftly intervened, capturing the suspect. Officers later found Magnolia safe in the bank manager's office. This charming kitten is now ready to find a loving and responsible family to call her own.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: How 'roadless rule' protects drinking water for 25M Americans
item.description: A new study reveals the Roadless Rule protects drinking water for twenty-five million Americans. This rule prevents development in national forests, safeguarding vital water sources. Removing protections could compromise water quality and increase treatment costs for utilities. These protected areas also support sensitive aquatic species and recreational opportunities. The U.S. Forest Service is currently reviewing public comments on the rule.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Japan’s abandoned rice fields are being restored into year-round wetlands to replenish groundwater and bring back frogs, dragonflies and endangered aquatic wildlife
item.description: Abandoned rice fields in Japan's Aso region are being restored into permanent wetlands to help refill groundwater and provide a safe habitat for frogs, dragonflies and other freshwater wildlife. The community-led project brings together local residents and researchers, showing how unused farmland can support biodiversity while helping restore water resources for the future.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Newborn kangaroos are born tiny, blind and embryo-like, but scientists say their arms develop early so they can crawl to the mother’s pouch and survive
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Andy Burnham set to be new UK Prime Minister, takes over from Keir Starmer on Monday
item.dc:creator: Prisha Patnaik

item.title: Quote of the day by William Gibson: 'The future is already here—it's just not evenly distributed'
item.description: William Gibson's observation emphasizes the disparate distribution of technological advancements in today's world. Innovations often emerge in isolated areas before reaching broader audiences. Access to these developments is influenced by available resources and educational opportunities, highlighting the ongoing challenge of achieving equitable progress. As we embrace change and strive for inclusive access, we can better shape a collective future for everyone.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Indian-origin man pleads guilty to manslaughter after fatal M4 crash in West London
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: US fugitive featured on 'America's Most Wanted' arrested after 20 years on the run
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 'Whoever kills Trump ...': Iran-backed Iraqi militia puts $10 million bounty on US president
item.dc:creator: Kanchan Yadav

item.title: A British island became known as the 'island of death' after a secret anthrax experiment and remained under quarantine for 48 years
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 'Constant onslaught': ICC staffer accuses chief prosecutor Karim Khan of sexual harassment
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Pirates allegedly hijack chemical tanker Asana in Gulf of Aden off Yemen
item.dc:creator: Vijaya Srivastava

item.title: Trump alleges China interference in US elections amid suspicion he's prepping for mid-term loss
item.description: President Donald Trump alleged China compromised American election data extensively. He claimed Beijing illicitly acquired millions of US voter files. Critics questioned these claims, citing public data availability and intelligence assessments. Intelligence agencies concluded China did not attempt to alter vote counts. The timing of these allegations raised concerns among Republicans and Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Chidanand Rajghatta

item.title: In 1980, Mount St Helens buried a river valley in volcanic sediment. Then 58 beavers helped turn the wasteland into a thriving wetland
item.description: More than 40 years after the Mount St. Helens eruption buried a Washington river valley under volcanic sediment, 58 relocated beavers have helped restore the damaged landscape. Their dams and wetlands brought back native plants, fish and wildlife. However, conservationists fear a planned government project to raise a sediment retention structure could undo years of natural restoration.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: From prison to paradise: Once a dreaded prison island, this remote haven in Greece is now protecting one of the world's rarest seals
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: China built “ghost” subway stations in empty fields before residents arrived, but years later new homes, roads and businesses began growing around them
item.description: China's empty subway stations were once seen as ghost cities and poor planning. However, this infrastructure development was a strategy to attract future urban growth. New roads and housing eventually surrounded these stations, transforming isolated areas. This approach involved building foundations before people arrived to stimulate development. Such ambitious projects carry significant financial burdens and potential vulnerabilities.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 'No beer in the cab': Florida woman stabs friend with scissors during argument
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk
